---
title: "16 · Mean Estimation from Samples (the LLN as MC's Engine)"
subtitle: "Sample averages converge to expected values — the foundation of every model-free RL algorithm."
lesson-id: "mfrl.mean-estimation-from-samples"
tier: "mfrl"
prerequisites: ["mfrl.truncated-policy-iteration", "t5.variance-clt"]
unlocks: []
applied-domains: ["ai-ml", "research"]
estimated-minutes: 30
status: "done"
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

1. Can you state the strong law of large numbers (LLN)?
2. Do you know what a [random variable](/glossary.qmd#random-variable) is
   and what it means to take its [expected value](/glossary.qmd#expected-value)?
3. Are you comfortable with the formula $\text{Var}(\bar X_n) = \sigma^2 / n$
   for the variance of a sample mean?
4. Can you state the [Central Limit Theorem](/glossary.qmd#central-limit-theorem)
   in words?

If shaky, revisit Tier 5 — Variance and CLT.
:::

## Touch

A weighted die has six faces with values $\{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\}$ and
non-uniform probabilities. Roll it. Take the running average. Watch the
average settle.

```{ojs}
viewof prob_button = Inputs.button("Add 50 more rolls")
```

```{ojs}
{
  const probs = [0.05, 0.10, 0.20, 0.25, 0.25, 0.15];
  const trueMean = probs.reduce((s, p, k) => s + (k + 1) * p, 0); // 3.85
  prob_button; // re-fire on click
  // Generate 50 rolls
  const sample = () => {
    const r = Math.random();
    let cum = 0;
    for (let i = 0; i < 6; i++) { cum += probs[i]; if (r < cum) return i + 1; }
    return 6;
  };
  if (!window.__mfrlRolls) window.__mfrlRolls = [];
  for (let i = 0; i < 50; i++) window.__mfrlRolls.push(sample());
  const rolls = window.__mfrlRolls;
  // Build running average sequence
  const runs = [];
  let total = 0;
  for (let i = 0; i < rolls.length; i++) {
    total += rolls[i];
    runs.push({ n: i + 1, avg: total / (i + 1) });
  }
  // Plot using simple SVG
  const w = 600, h = 250, ml = 50, mr = 30, mt = 20, mb = 30;
  const xs = n => ml + (n - 1) / Math.max(1, runs.length - 1) * (w - ml - mr);
  const ys = a => h - mb - (a - 1) / 5 * (h - mt - mb);
  const path = "M " + runs.map(r => `${xs(r.n)},${ys(r.avg)}`).join(" L ");
  const trueLine = `M ${ml},${ys(trueMean)} L ${w - mr},${ys(trueMean)}`;
  return html`<div>
    <svg width=${w} height=${h} style="background:#fafafa; border:1px solid #ccc;">
      <path d=${trueLine} stroke="red" stroke-dasharray="4,4" fill="none"/>
      <path d=${path} stroke="steelblue" stroke-width="1.5" fill="none"/>
      <text x=${w - mr - 100} y=${ys(trueMean) - 5} fill="red" font-size="11">true mean ${trueMean}</text>
      <text x=${ml} y=${h - 5} font-size="11">n = number of rolls (current: ${runs.length})</text>
      <text x=${5} y=${ys(1)} font-size="11">1</text>
      <text x=${5} y=${ys(6)} font-size="11">6</text>
    </svg>
    <div style="font-size:0.9rem; color:#666; margin-top:0.5rem;">Running average → true mean (red dashed) as n grows.</div>
  </div>`;
}
```

Press the button repeatedly. With 50 rolls the average wobbles. With 500
it's close to the red line. With 5000 it's basically locked. **You did not
need to know the true probabilities** — the samples carried the information.

## Wonder

1. The dotted red line is the *true mean* — but you computed your average
   without ever knowing the probabilities. How did the samples tell you?
2. With 100 rolls you're "close" to the mean. With 10,000 rolls you're
   *much* closer. By how much closer? Twice? Ten times? A hundred times?
3. What if the die's distribution had a long tail — values up to 1000
   with tiny probability? Would the running average still converge?

## Discover

### Problem 1 — The strong law of large numbers

State the strong LLN, then explain in plain English what it promises and
what it does *not* promise.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
"Almost surely" is the key phrase. Look up the precise statement.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Strong LLN.** Let $X_1, X_2, \ldots$ be i.i.d.\ random variables with
$\mathbb{E}|X_1| < \infty$ and mean $\mu$. Then
$$\bar X_n := \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n X_i \xrightarrow{a.s.} \mu \quad \text{as } n \to \infty.$$

**What it promises.** With probability 1, the running sample average will
eventually settle to $\mu$ and stay close.

**What it does *not* promise.** A speed of convergence. A bound on the
error at any finite $n$. Convergence in any metric stronger than "almost
sure." (For speed and error bounds, you need the CLT or concentration
inequalities like Hoeffding.)
</details>

### Problem 2 — Variance of the sample mean

If $X_i$ are i.i.d.\ with variance $\sigma^2$, show that
$\text{Var}(\bar X_n) = \sigma^2 / n$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$\text{Var}(\sum X_i) = \sum \text{Var}(X_i)$ when independent.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\text{Var}(\bar X_n) = \text{Var}((1/n)\sum X_i) = (1/n^2) \text{Var}(\sum X_i)
= (1/n^2)\sum \text{Var}(X_i) = (1/n^2) \cdot n\sigma^2 = \sigma^2/n.$

**Standard deviation** scales as $\sigma/\sqrt n$. To halve the typical
error you need *four times* as many samples. To shrink it by 10x, you need
*100x*. **MC convergence is slow** — but it is universal.
</details>

### Problem 3 — Sample size for a target accuracy

You want to estimate $\mu$ within $\pm 0.01$ with 95% confidence, given
$\sigma \approx 1$. How many samples?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
By CLT, $\bar X_n - \mu \approx \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2/n)$. The 95% CI
half-width is $1.96 \sigma/\sqrt n$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Want $1.96 \sigma/\sqrt n \le 0.01$. Solving with $\sigma = 1$:
$\sqrt n \ge 196 \Rightarrow n \ge 196^2 = 38{,}416$.

**Tens of thousands of samples for two-decimal accuracy.** This is why MC
RL needs lots of episodes. (And why we'll spend Lessons 20-22 on
*stochastic approximation* tricks to do better.)
</details>

### Problem 4 — Apply to RL: estimate the value of a state

In an MDP, the value $v_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi[G_t \mid S_t = s]$. Suppose
you generate $n$ trajectories starting at $s$ and following $\pi$, each
yielding a return $G^{(i)}$. Show that the sample average
$\hat v_n(s) = (1/n)\sum_i G^{(i)}$ converges to $v_\pi(s)$ a.s.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
The $G^{(i)}$ are i.i.d.\ samples from the *return distribution* under
$\pi$ starting at $s$. Apply LLN.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Let $G$ be the (random) return $\sum_{t=0}^\infty \gamma^t R_t$ with the
agent following $\pi$ from initial state $s$. By definition,
$\mathbb{E}[G] = v_\pi(s)$. The $G^{(i)}$ are i.i.d.\ draws of $G$, so by
LLN, $\hat v_n(s) \to v_\pi(s)$ almost surely.

**You did not need to know $P$, $r$, or anything about the MDP** — only
the ability to *generate trajectories* and observe returns.

This is the entire content of "model-free RL." Everything from Lesson 17
onward applies it in increasingly clever ways.
</details>

### Problem 5 — Variance of the return estimate

Suppose returns under $\pi$ from $s$ have variance $\sigma_G^2(s)$. After
$n$ trajectories from $s$, what's the standard error of $\hat v_n(s)$?
For our running die-roll widget (1 to 6 values, $\sigma_G \approx 1.5$),
how many trajectories for $\pm 0.05$ accuracy?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\text{SE}(\hat v_n(s)) = \sigma_G(s) / \sqrt n$. For
$\sigma_G = 1.5$, $\pm 0.05$ at 95% CI: $1.96 \cdot 1.5/\sqrt n \le 0.05
\Rightarrow n \ge (1.96 \cdot 1.5/0.05)^2 = 3457$.

In RL, $\sigma_G$ can be huge — particularly in environments with sparse
rewards or long horizons. This is the *high-variance problem* of MC RL,
and is what motivates TD methods (Lessons 23+).
</details>

::: {.key-insight}
"Expectation = sample average in the limit" is the single mathematical
move that lets us do RL without a model. Every algorithm from here to the
end of the track is a clever variation on this theme: how to extract
maximum information from minimum samples.
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

**[Monte Carlo estimation](/glossary.qmd#monte-carlo-estimation).** To
estimate $\mathbb{E}[X]$ for a random variable $X$ that you can sample
from, draw $n$ i.i.d.\ samples $X_1, \ldots, X_n$ and compute
$$\bar X_n = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n X_i.$$
By the strong LLN, $\bar X_n \xrightarrow{a.s.} \mathbb{E}[X]$ as $n \to \infty$,
with standard error $\sigma/\sqrt n$ where $\sigma^2 = \text{Var}(X)$.

In RL, this is used to estimate $v_\pi(s)$ and $q_\pi(s, a)$ from sampled
trajectories: $\hat v_\pi(s) = (1/n)\sum_i G^{(i)}$ where each $G^{(i)}$
is the discounted return of a trajectory from $s$ under $\pi$.

:::

A verse:

> *Average enough samples,*
> *And the truth shows through.*
> *No model required —*
> *Only the patience to roll the die.*

## Apply

### Worked example — Estimate $\mathbb{E}[\text{Bernoulli}(0.3)]$

Generate $n$ samples from $\text{Bernoulli}(0.3)$ — each $X_i$ is 1 with
probability 0.3, else 0. Estimate $\mu = 0.3$ from samples.

**$n = 10$.** Suppose we draw 0, 1, 0, 0, 1, 0, 0, 0, 1, 0. Sum = 3.
$\hat\mu_{10} = 0.30$. (Could easily have been 0.1 or 0.5.)
SE = $\sqrt{p(1-p)/n} = \sqrt{0.21/10} \approx 0.145$. 95% CI: $\hat\mu \pm 0.28$.
**Useless precision** — the CI spans most of $[0, 1]$.

**$n = 100$.** SE $\approx 0.046$. 95% CI: $\hat\mu \pm 0.09$. Decent for a
quick check.

**$n = 1000$.** SE $\approx 0.014$. 95% CI: $\hat\mu \pm 0.029$. Good.

**$n = 10{,}000$.** SE $\approx 0.0046$. 95% CI: $\hat\mu \pm 0.009$.
Production-quality.

Notice: every 100x in $n$ buys 10x in precision. The $1/\sqrt n$ rate is
inescapable. (For a Bernoulli, you can do exactly the variance because
$\sigma^2 = p(1-p)$ is known; for general distributions you'd estimate
$\sigma^2$ from the sample too.)

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** A coin you suspect is fair shows heads on 502 of 1000 flips.
Construct a 95% CI for the head probability $p$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$\hat p = 0.502$, $\text{SE} = \sqrt{\hat p (1 - \hat p)/n}$. 95% CI:
$\hat p \pm 1.96 \cdot \text{SE}$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\text{SE} = \sqrt{0.502 \cdot 0.498 / 1000} \approx 0.0158$. 95% CI:
$0.502 \pm 0.031 = [0.471, 0.533]$. **The CI contains 0.5.** No evidence
the coin is biased — $1000$ flips is barely enough to detect a 3% bias.
</details>

**W2.** You want to estimate $\mathbb{E}[X]$ where $X \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1)$
to within $\pm 0.01$ at 99% confidence. How many samples?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
99% z-score is 2.576. Solve $2.576/\sqrt n \le 0.01$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\sqrt n \ge 257.6 \Rightarrow n \ge 66{,}358$. **Tightening confidence
from 95% to 99% nearly doubles the required sample size**, even though
$\sigma$ is unchanged.
</details>

**W3.** A long-tail distribution has mean 5 but $\sigma = 100$. How many
samples to estimate the mean to $\pm 0.5$ at 95% confidence?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$1.96 \cdot 100/\sqrt n \le 0.5 \Rightarrow n \ge (392)^2 = 153{,}664$.
**150,000 samples for one-decimal precision** when variance is large.
This is why heavy-tailed RL environments (e.g.\ deep exploration with
sparse rewards) are so sample-hungry.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** Show that the *empirical variance*
$\hat\sigma^2 = (1/n)\sum (X_i - \bar X)^2$ converges a.s.\ to the true
variance $\sigma^2$. (You can use LLN twice.)

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$(1/n)\sum X_i^2 \to \mathbb{E}[X^2]$ and $\bar X \to \mathbb{E}[X]$ both
by LLN.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$(1/n)\sum (X_i - \bar X)^2 = (1/n)\sum X_i^2 - \bar X^2$. By LLN,
$(1/n)\sum X_i^2 \to \mathbb{E}[X^2]$ a.s.\ and $\bar X \to \mathbb{E}[X]$
a.s. Hence $\hat\sigma^2 \to \mathbb{E}[X^2] - \mathbb{E}[X]^2 = \sigma^2$
a.s. **You can estimate the unknown variance from the same samples** — no
extra information needed.
</details>

**P2.** The *Hoeffding inequality* says that for $X_i \in [a, b]$ i.i.d.,
$$P(|\bar X_n - \mu| \ge t) \le 2 \exp(-2nt^2/(b - a)^2).$$
Use this to find $n$ for $|\bar X_n - \mu| \le 0.01$ with probability
$\ge 0.99$, given $X_i \in [0, 1]$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Want $2\exp(-2n \cdot 0.0001/1) \le 0.01$. Solve for $n$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$2\exp(-0.0002 n) \le 0.01 \Rightarrow \exp(-0.0002 n) \le 0.005
\Rightarrow 0.0002 n \ge \log(200) \approx 5.3 \Rightarrow n \ge 26{,}500$.

**Compare to the variance-based bound.** With $\sigma \le 0.5$ for
$X \in [0, 1]$, the variance approach gives
$n \ge (2.576 \cdot 0.5/0.01)^2 \approx 16{,}600$. Hoeffding is *looser*
than CLT but is a *finite-sample* guarantee, not an asymptotic one — and
it doesn't require knowing $\sigma$.
</details>

**P3.** *Estimating $v_\pi(s)$ via Monte Carlo rollouts.* You're given an
MDP with $\gamma = 0.9$ and a policy $\pi$. You generate 1000 trajectories
from state $s$, each truncated at $T = 100$ steps. The empirical mean
return is $\hat v = 4.7$ with empirical standard deviation $\hat\sigma_G = 2.1$.
Construct a 95% CI for $v_\pi(s)$ and discuss the truncation bias.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**CI.** $\hat v \pm 1.96 \cdot 2.1/\sqrt{1000} = 4.7 \pm 0.13 = [4.57, 4.83]$.

**Truncation bias.** True return is over infinite horizon; you stopped at
$T = 100$. Bias $\le \gamma^T \cdot R_{\max}/(1 - \gamma)$. For
$\gamma = 0.9, T = 100$: $0.9^{100} \approx 2.6 \times 10^{-5}$, so bias is
negligible (assuming bounded rewards). **For $\gamma = 0.99$ and $T = 100$,
$\gamma^T \approx 0.37$ — bias would dominate the CI.** Always check this.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** *Importance sampling intro.* You want $\mathbb{E}_p[f(X)]$ but
can only sample from $q$, not $p$. Show that
$\mathbb{E}_p[f(X)] = \mathbb{E}_q[(p(X)/q(X)) f(X)]$, and propose an
estimator from $q$-samples.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$\int f(x) p(x) dx = \int f(x) (p(x)/q(x)) q(x) dx$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\mathbb{E}_p[f(X)] = \int f(x) p(x) dx = \int f(x) (p(x)/q(x)) q(x) dx = \mathbb{E}_q[(p(X)/q(X)) f(X)]$.

Estimator: $\hat\theta = (1/n) \sum_i (p(X_i)/q(X_i)) f(X_i)$ with
$X_i \sim q$. **This is the foundation of off-policy RL** — Lesson 37
develops it for actor-critic. The variance can blow up if $p/q$ is large,
which is why off-policy methods are subtle.
</details>

**S2.** Suppose you can sample $X$ but the variance is enormous. *Control
variates*: find a related random variable $Y$ with known mean $\mu_Y$ and
high correlation with $X$. Show that $\hat\mu_X^{cv} = \bar X - c(\bar Y - \mu_Y)$
is unbiased for $\mathbb{E}[X]$ and find the optimal $c$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\mathbb{E}[\hat\mu_X^{cv}] = \mathbb{E}[X] - c(\mathbb{E}[Y] - \mu_Y) = \mathbb{E}[X]$
(unbiased). Variance:
$\text{Var}(\hat\mu_X^{cv}) = \text{Var}(\bar X) + c^2 \text{Var}(\bar Y) - 2c \text{Cov}(\bar X, \bar Y)$.
Minimise over $c$: $c^* = \text{Cov}(X, Y)/\text{Var}(Y)$, giving
$\text{Var}(\hat\mu_X^{cv}) = \text{Var}(\bar X)(1 - \rho^2)$ where $\rho$
is correlation. **Variance reduction by factor $1 - \rho^2$.** This is
the seed of *advantage* in actor-critic (Lesson 36): subtracting a baseline
correlated with the return reduces gradient variance.
</details>

::: {.fun-fact}
Stanislaw Ulam invented the Monte Carlo method in 1946 while recovering
from encephalitis at Los Alamos. He was playing Solitaire and wondered
what fraction of layouts were winnable. Combinatorial enumeration was
hopeless, but he realised: **just play many random games and count**.
With John von Neumann and Nicholas Metropolis, he developed the idea into
a method for nuclear simulations. The name "Monte Carlo" came from
Metropolis, after the casino — Ulam's uncle was a chronic gambler.
:::

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"Sample average is always close to the mean."** Only *eventually*.
   For small $n$, it can be far off — and the speed of convergence is
   $1/\sqrt n$, which is slow.
2. **"More samples = more accuracy, linearly."** No — accuracy improves
   like $1/\sqrt n$. To halve error you need 4× the samples.
3. **"LLN works for any distribution."** Almost — you need
   $\mathbb{E}|X| < \infty$. The Cauchy distribution has no mean, so its
   sample averages do not converge.
4. **"You need to know the variance to use MC."** No — you can estimate
   it from the sample (P1), and Hoeffding (P2) gives bounds without it.
:::

### Takeaways

1. **LLN.** Sample averages converge a.s.\ to expectations.
2. **$1/\sqrt n$ rate.** Standard error scales as $\sigma/\sqrt n$;
   accuracy improves *slowly* with sample size.
3. **CLT** gives confidence intervals for finite $n$; **Hoeffding**
   gives finite-sample bounds without knowing $\sigma$.
4. **In RL.** $\hat v_\pi(s) = $ average of trajectory returns from $s$.
   No model needed — only the ability to roll out.

### Self-Assessment

1. Could you derive the standard error $\sigma/\sqrt n$ from scratch?
2. Did you compute at least 2 sample-size questions (W2, W3) without
   peeking past Hint 1?
3. Can you state the difference between CLT and Hoeffding bounds?

- **3 yes:** move on to Lesson 17 (MC Basic).
- **2 yes:** redo P3 — the truncation-bias point is critical for real
  RL implementations.
- **0–1 yes:** revisit Tier 5 *Variance and CLT*.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **Tier 5 — Variance and CLT.** Provides LLN, CLT, and the
  $\sigma/\sqrt n$ scaling.
- **Lesson 15 (Truncated PI).** Establishes the evaluate-improve template
  whose evaluation step we'll now replace with sample averages.

### What this unlocks

- **Lesson 17 — MC Basic.** Replaces PI's exact evaluation with MC
  averages of returns.
- **Lessons 18-19 — MC Exploring Starts, $\varepsilon$-greedy.** Refine
  MC Basic for sample efficiency and exploration.
- **Lessons 20-22 — Stochastic approximation.** Generalise MC: solve
  fixed-point problems from noisy samples (Robbins-Monro).
- **Lessons 23-26 — TD methods.** Combine MC with bootstrapping for
  variance reduction.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / ML.** Every model-free RL algorithm in production — DQN, PPO, SAC,
AlphaZero (when trained from self-play) — is fundamentally a sample
estimator of an expectation. The LLN is the proof that they can possibly
work; the CLT is the engine of every confidence interval reported in RL
papers.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Research / numerical computation.** Monte Carlo integration is the only
practical way to compute high-dimensional integrals. Particle physics,
financial option pricing, Bayesian inference (MCMC), molecular
simulations — all rest on the LLN.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Sister synthesis lesson.** [`t7.rl-theory`](../../tier-7-masters-tracks/06-rl-theory/index.qmd)
gives the single-paragraph view of reinforcement learning. The
"sample-based" turn this lesson opens is the second half of paragraph one.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

- Source: Shiyu Zhao, *Mathematical Foundations of Reinforcement Learning*
  (Springer 2025), §5.1. Cite freely; never transcribe.
- Touchstones: 3Blue1Brown's CLT episode (intuition for $1/\sqrt n$),
  Better Explained ("expectation is the long-run average" framing),
  Sutton & Barto Ch. 5 (the "MC Basic" plan that begins next lesson).
:::
